questions.question: Why do raw LLMs close bargains at such low rates (~27%), and why doesn't scale fix it?
options: They lack negotiation vocabulary

They conflate deciding the offer with narrating the offer — breaking bargaining rules, ignoring the ZOPA, anchoring poorly; bigger models just produce more-plausible language with similar strategic error
Providers block negotiation use cases
Context windows are too small for offers
questions.explanation: "Measuring Bargaining Abilities" found GPT-4 isn't structurally better at bargaining than GPT-3.5 — better at the language of bargaining only. Buyer-side is measurably harder than seller-side.

questions.question: What is the OG-Narrator decomposition, and what did it achieve?
options: Two LLMs negotiating against each other
A deterministic offer generator computes numeric moves (Rubinstein/Zeuthen/tit-for-tat) while the LLM only narrates — deal rate jumped from 26.67% to 88.88%
An audio narrator for accessibility
A fine-tuned negotiation model
questions.explanation: Decoupling mechanism from communication: prices stay in the bargaining zone, anchors are strategic not emotional, and the LLM does what it's good at — writing. The classical multi-agent lesson, rediscovered.

questions.question: How does Contract Net Protocol work, and what is its modern relevance?
options: It is a blockchain smart-contract standard

Manager broadcasts a call-for-proposals, bidders propose, manager accepts one and rejects the rest — Smith's 1980 mechanism, FIPA-codified, now the template for LLM-powered task markets with the LLM in the narration slot
A legal e-signature protocol
A network handshake for agents
questions.explanation: cfp → propose → accept/reject-proposal is the reference task-market mechanism. Plug a deterministic auction core under LLM narration and you get the modern version of a 45-year-old design.

questions.question: What did the ~180k-negotiation competition find about winning strategies?
options: The largest model always won

Chain-of-thought-CONCEALING agents won — printing "my reservation price is $70" into a visible scratchpad hands the opponent your strategy; winners compute privately and emit only the offer plus minimal narration
Random offers beat all strategies
Politeness was the strongest predictor
questions.explanation: Visible reasoning is exploitable reasoning. The competition result formalizes an operational rule: separate the private strategy channel from the public message channel.

questions.question: What do NegotiationArena's findings imply about deploying cooperative agents?
options: Cooperative agents always win on fairness metrics
Persona manipulation moves payoffs ~20%, fair agents get exploited by adversarial ones without explicit counter-posturing, and symmetric pairs still reach inequitable outcomes ~40% of the time
Negotiation between LLMs is always fair
Adversarial agents are banned by providers
questions.explanation: LLMs negotiate too much like humans — including the exploitable parts. A deployed negotiating agent needs defensive structure, not just good intentions; Bhattacharya et al. even measured personality differences (Llama-3 most effective, Claude-3 aggressive, GPT-4 fairest).
